summary: Japanese ukiyo-e woodblock print artist of the Utagawa school, considered the last great ukiyo-e master. Most famous for The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido and One Hundred Famous Views of Edo. Van Gogh copied his prints. Also created series on the Kiso Kaido, Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji, and bird-and-flower prints. Inspired Szymborska's poetry collection The People on the Bridge.

works.name: The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido (1833–1834)
works.description: Woodblock print series depicting locations along the Tokaido road from Edo to Kyoto. Begins with Nihonbashi Bridge. Includes depictions of Kanagawa, Kambara, and Shono (rain scene). Final print shows carts and travelers crossing a river arriving at Kyoto. Wikipedia

images.caption: Mishima station, from the Fifty-three Stations of the Tokaido

examples: Three boats are seen without sails on the far left of one depiction of this entity, and four more ships sail behind a row of merchant's booths in that work

works.name: One Hundred Famous Views of Edo (1856–1858)
works.description: Series of 119 woodblock prints depicting scenes from Edo. Includes Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake (1857) and Plum Park in Kameido (1857). Van Gogh copied several of these. Finished after Hiroshige's death by his student who adopted his name. Also includes a view of Kambara in snow and plums at Kamada.

images.caption: Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake

clues.clue: Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake: thin slanted black lines represent rain over a bridge; people run for cover; Van Gogh copied as Bridge in the Rain, painting faux calligraphy around the print
examples: uses many thin, slanted, black lines to represent a sudden rain over a bridge
people on a wooden bridge run for cover during a sudden shower

examples: features a prominent horizontal branch and red hue, which inspired elements in Paul Gauguin's Vision After the Sermon and was copied by Vincent Van Gogh
shows the abstracted branches of a tree nicknamed "Sleeping Dragon"
An 1857 print copied by Vincent van Gogh shows a deep red sky obscured by one of these plants nicknamed the 'Sleeping Dragon' at an estate in Kameido

comprehensive_summary:
Utagawa Hiroshige (1797–1858) was a Japanese woodblock print artist of the Utagawa school, considered the last great master of ukiyo-e. His most celebrated series is The Fifty-Three Stations of the Tokaido (1833–1834), 53 prints depicting post stations along the Tokaido road from Edo to Kyoto. The series begins at Nihonbashi Bridge, includes dramatic scenes at stations like Kanagawa, Kambara, and Shono (where a servant removes his jacket to cover his master in a rainstorm), and concludes with carts and travelers crossing a river to reach the imperial capital.

His One Hundred Famous Views of Edo (1856–1858) is equally important. The print Sudden Shower over Shin-Ohashi Bridge and Atake (1857) uses thin diagonal black lines to convey driving rain over a wooden bridge as figures cower with umbrellas; Vincent van Gogh copied it as Bridge in the Rain and painted faux Japanese calligraphy around the border. Plum Park in Kameido (1857) depicts the abstracted, portrait-oriented branches of a plum tree nicknamed the "Sleeping Dragon" against a deep red sky; Van Gogh also copied this print, and its prominent horizontal branch and red hue are believed to have inspired the red field in Gauguin's Vision After the Sermon. The series also includes plum orchard views at Kameido and Kamada, and was completed after Hiroshige's death by his student, who adopted his name.

Beyond these two landmark series, Hiroshige produced a 69-station series on the Kiso Kaido road, a set of Thirty-Six Views of Mount Fuji (paralleling Hokusai's famous series), and hundreds of bird-and-flower prints including Yellow Rose and Frogs. He was parodied by fellow Utagawa artist Kuniyoshi, who used cats with punny names to reference his works. His influence on Western art was profound: his style shaped Japonism broadly, and the Polish Nobel laureate Wislawa Szymborska drew the title of her poetry collection The People on the Bridge from one of his prints.
